nativeRange.habitat: Shallow rice paddies, slow-moving streams, ponds, coastal ditches, and tidal estuaries; heavily vegetated surface-oriented environments with soft to moderate current

decorPreferences: floating plants (water lettuce, duckweed, frogbit) for egg attachment and shade
fine-leaved stem plants (water sprite, hornwort) for cover and spawning substrate
gentle filtration — sponge filter preferred to avoid surface turbulence

diet.notes: Accepts a wide range of small foods at the surface: high-quality micro-pellets, crushed flake, frozen or live baby brine shrimp, daphnia, cyclops, and small bloodworms. Grazes on algae and biofilm. Mouth is small and upturned — food size matters; particles should be 0.5–1 mm or smaller. Will also eat their own eggs and fry if not separated.

goodWith: other medaka strains and color forms
dwarf shrimp (neocaridina, caridina) — adults safe, fry at risk
avoidWith: larger fish that can swallow medaka whole
fast aggressive feeders that outcompete surface feeding

imageCandidates.notes: Wild-type medaka lateral view. FishBase standard species image.
Wild-type medaka in aquarium setting; clear lateral view.

summary: The Medaka Rice Fish is a tiny, hardy East Asian killifish famed as a premier model organism in developmental biology and genetics — Japan’s equivalent of the zebrafish. Exceptionally cold-tolerant (0–30°C) and euryhaline, it thrives unheated and in brackish conditions. Dozens of selectively-bred color forms exist. Easy surface-dwelling egg-attacher ideal for beginners.
careNotes: Medaka are among the hardiest aquarium fish available, capable of tolerating temperatures from near-freezing to 30°C — making them suitable for unheated indoor tanks and outdoor tubs in temperate climates. A 40 L tank is sufficient for a small shoal of 6–10 fish; longer tanks with ample surface area are preferred over tall ones. Being surface dwellers with an upturned mouth, they feed at the waterline and require gentle filtration to prevent strong surface current. A tightly fitting lid or lowered water line is advisable as medaka are capable jumpers. Floating plants such as water lettuce, frogbit, or duckweed serve a dual purpose: providing shade and diffuse lighting preferred by the fish, and offering spawning substrate to which egg clusters adhere. Medaka are euryhaline — tolerant of fresh, brackish, and even low-salinity marine conditions — making them adaptable to a wide range of setups. They have been kept successfully at SG 1.000–1.005 without issue. Dozens of selectively-bred color strains are commercially available, including Daisy, Hi-Light, Black Diamond, Platinum, Orange/Yuki, Blue, and many others; most share identical care requirements. As a storied research model organism used extensively in Japanese developmental biology and genetics (comparable to zebrafish in Western science), medaka have been studied aboard the International Space Station. Wild populations in Japan are listed as Near Threatened due to agricultural habitat loss.
breedingNotes: Medaka are prolific and easy to breed — one of the most beginner-friendly egg-scattering species in the hobby. After morning courtship, females carry a cluster of eggs (typically 5–30) suspended from their vent by adhesive filaments for several hours before rubbing them off onto fine-leaved plants or spawning mops. Eggs are transparent, large relative to the parent’s size, and visible to the naked eye. At 25°C, eggs hatch in approximately 10–14 days; at cooler temperatures hatching may take 3–4 weeks. Fry are free-swimming immediately and can accept infusoria, commercially prepared fry food, or finely powdered flake from day one. Adults will consume eggs and fry; removing egg-bearing plants or spawning mops to a separate rearing vessel significantly improves yield. No specialised water chemistry or conditioning is required to trigger spawning — a photoperiod of 14+ hours of light (natural or artificial) is the primary spawning cue.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Oryzias latipes
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 4 cm), temperature range (4–28°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness, native range (Japan, China, Korea, Vietnam), habitat (rice paddies, ponds, ditches, streams), diet, breeding behaviour (egg-attaching, filament eggs, 10–14 day hatch at 25°C), compatibility, and general care notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Oryzias latipes
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 4.5 cm TL), temperature (4–30°C), native range, taxonomy (Adrianichthyidae, Beloniformes), lifespan, and euryhaline/salinity tolerance data. Confirmed research-model organism status and IUCN Near Threatened (Japan) note.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Medaka Rice Fish Care Guide
additional.notes: Confirmed tank size recommendation (10 gal / ~40 L minimum), surface-feeding behaviour, floating plant preference, cold-water hardiness, colour strain overview (Orange, Daisy, Hi-Light, Black Diamond, Platinum), and beginner suitability. Noted 14+ hour photoperiod as spawning trigger.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Medaka (Oryzias latipes)
additional.notes: Supplementary source for European hobbyist perspective; confirmed euryhaline tolerance, cold-water capability, shoaling behaviour, surface-dwelling nature, egg-cluster adhesion to plants, and availability of numerous selectively-bred colour forms in the trade.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — The Medaka: Japan’s Model Fish
additional.notes: Provided historical and scientific context: medaka as Japan’s premier vertebrate model organism, ISS microgravity experiments, genetic tractability, transparent embryos, and hobbyist history in Japan (kept for centuries). Supplementary caresheet data consistent with other sources.

fish.breedingNotes: Prolific egg-attacher; females carry adhesive egg clusters before depositing them on fine plants or mops. Eggs hatch in 10–14 days at 25°C. Long photoperiod (14+ h) is the primary spawning trigger. Adults eat eggs and fry; separate spawning mops or plants into rearing vessel for best yield. Fry accept powdered fry food from day one.
